title: Focaccia with tomatoes and olives
subtitle: Dimpled Italian flatbread with olive oil topping
excerpt: A deeply olive-oiled Italian flatbread with cherry tomatoes and olives pressed into the surface. The dough is wet, requires no kneading, and is developed with stretch-and-fold folds during a long room-temperature ferment.
sourceNotes: Focaccia genovese is the original: a dimpled, olive-oil-rich flatbread from Liguria. The formula is documented in Italian domestic cookery and in professional baking texts throughout the twentieth century. This version uses a high-hydration, no-knead dough developed with stretch-and-fold during bulk fermentation.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cut portions. Reheat from frozen at 180°C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day it is baked. The dough can be refrigerated overnight after the first fold and baked the next day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, top shelf — high heat gives a crisp base and a golden, blistered top

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique for developing gluten in a wet dough without traditional kneading. During bulk fermentation, you stretch one side of the dough up and fold it over the centre, rotating the bowl and repeating four times. This builds structure and distributes the gases of fermentation without degassing the dough.

content.text: Focaccia dough is deliberately wet (80% hydration), which is why it flows rather than holds its shape. No kneading is needed; the gluten develops during bulk fermentation through a series of stretch-and-fold sets. The abundant olive oil is not decoration: it creates the characteristic crisp, almost fried underside.

Combine the {{strong-white-flour}}, {{fine-sea-salt}}, and {{instant-yeast}} in a large bowl. Add the 80 ml of {{olive-oil}} and the {{water}}. Mix until no dry flour remains — the dough will be rough and shaggy. Cover and leave for 30 minutes.
Bulk ferment with stretch and fold, 2 hours
at 30-minute intervals over the first 2 hours. Between sets, cover the bowl. After the final fold, the dough should be smooth and hold its shape briefly when pulled.

Pour 4 tablespoons of the remaining {{olive-oil}} into a 30×40 cm tin and spread it to coat the base and sides. Tip the dough into the tin and turn it to coat in oil. Gently stretch the dough to fill the tin — do not force it; it will relax with time. Drizzle over the rest of the oil. Leave uncovered for 45 minutes until puffed.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. With oiled fingers, press deep dimples all over the dough — push all the way to the base of the tin. Press the {{cherry-tomatoes}} and {{olives}} into the dimples. Scatter over the {{flaked-sea-salt}} and {{fresh-rosemary}} if using. Bake for 20–25 minutes until deep golden and crisp on the base. Drizzle with a little extra olive oil as it comes out of the oven.
